target: WIN-060 detail_verdict_text — restructure post-hoc paragraph per ISS-1866. Currently leads with monitor.py-absence ("test inapplicable"), giving a defender the opening "you penalize us for not monitoring; the unmonitored claim is still consistent with observation." Curmudgeon WIN-060.c3 hole #2 (moderate): the disqualifier is that westward drift is a 390-year-old public observable — adopting it as a 2026 prediction cannot be a fresh prediction under the legitimate-novelty carve-out (Part 3.1b); monitor.py absence is a symptom, not the cause.
related_prior_expansion: EXP-294 was the previous WIN-060-area expansion (Timestamp Error tab harmonization, ISS-1875). The c3 review post-dated the c4/c5 patch series but the templated post-hoc paragraph predates all of these — it is the original Part 3.1b boilerplate first introduced in the post-hoc audit sweep and replicated across monitoring=none WINs.

decision.rationale: Option A (reorder the paragraph so the public-availability/age-of-observable point leads, the legitimate-novelty carve-out is named as the explicit disqualifier, and monitor.py absence is demoted to "mechanical confirmation / symptom") is correct because: (1) it directly addresses the curmudgeon-identified circularity — the paragraph no longer rests on "absent monitoring → test inapplicable → claim disqualified," it now rests on "the data is too old and too public to be a fresh prediction → carve-out fails → claim disqualified, and by the way no monitoring exists either"; (2) it preserves all factual claims (NOAA/ESA citations, dome-author-self-definition framing, Part 3.1b reference); (3) it removes the defender opening "you penalize us for not monitoring; the unmonitored claim is still consistent with observation" — the answer is no longer "we cannot apply the test" but "the test would fail anyway because the observation has been public for 390 years"; (4) the structural impossibility (a 390-year-old observable cannot be a 2026 prediction) is now load-bearing, with the timestamping mechanics demoted to corroboration.
decision.why_not_option_b: Option B (delete the post-hoc paragraph entirely for WIN-060 since the scientific critique already does the work) was considered but rejected because: (1) the post-hoc audit is part of our cross-cutting "are these predictions or rationalizations?" thesis and should fire on every WIN it can — silently dropping it for WIN-060 would weaken the cross-WIN consistency story; (2) the disqualifier IS load-bearing here — without the post-hoc paragraph the verdict reduces to "Std Model Explains" alone, which is weaker than "the dome cannot derive the rate AND is not actually predicting it (390-year-old observable)"; (3) the curmudgeon recommendation was "reorder," not "remove."
decision.why_not_option_c: Option C (keep the original ordering but add a single rebuttal sentence at the bottom) was considered but rejected because the structural problem is that the paragraph reads like the conclusion follows from monitor.py absence; bolting a rebuttal at the end leaves the original misordering as the rhetorical primary. Reordering is the load-bearing fix; appending is cosmetic.

patches.uniqueness_verified: Substring count = 1 in current data/wins.json WIN-060.detail_verdict_text (verified 2026-05-09 against origin/main HEAD).
patches.find: Independent of the scientific critique above, this claim also fails our post-hoc test. The confirming value comes from a field-specific published dataset (IGRF-14 / NOAA WMM2025 — derived from the INTERMAGNET observatory network) that was publicly available at the time of the dome monitor commit, and we could not locate a pre-registration of the expected value in a timestamping venue independent of the author. Under the dome author's own definition ("prediction was timestamped before confirming data was pulled"), no monitor.py entry exists for this value at all — the dome author has not included it in the monitoring suite — making even the weaker commit-timestamp test inapplicable: there is nothing to timestamp before the data arrives. The legitimate-novelty carve-out of Part 3.1b requires that the data be "not plausibly known to the author at write-time," which is not the case for indexed public datasets.
patches.replace: Independent of the scientific critique above, this claim also fails our post-hoc test. Westward magnetic drift has been a published observable since Edmund Halley's late-17th-century declination surveys — roughly 390 years of public record — and the modern rate is tabulated to high precision in NOAA's WMM2025 and ESA's IGRF-14 (derived from the INTERMAGNET observatory network and ESA Swarm L1B magnetometer data), all publicly indexed years before the V51 dome monitor commit. The legitimate-novelty carve-out of Part 3.1b requires that the data be "not plausibly known to the author at write-time," which a 390-year-old indexed observable plainly cannot satisfy — adopting it as a 2026 prediction is the disqualifier, not the absence of a timestamp. Mechanically, no monitor.py entry exists for this value — the dome author has not included it in the monitoring suite — so even the weaker commit-timestamp test that the dome author proposes ("prediction was timestamped before confirming data was pulled") has nothing to anchor; the monitoring absence is a symptom of the disqualifier, not the disqualifier itself.

cross_page_consistency_after_patches: No cross-page implications. The post-hoc paragraph appears only in data/wins.json (per-WIN detail_verdict_text); docs/index.html is generated from this source. No mirror in data/sections.json, no mirror in build-scripts/. The Part 3.1b anchor (#posthoc-standard) is preserved unchanged.

holes_addressed.hole: WIN-060.c3 hole #2 (moderate): post-hoc paragraph argument structure is partially circular for monitoring=none WINs.
holes_addressed.how_addressed: Reordering: lead sentence is now the 390-year public-observable claim, second sentence is the carve-out disqualification, third sentence is the demoted monitor.py-absence (now framed as "symptom of the disqualifier, not the disqualifier itself").
holes_addressed.defender_test: Defender argues: "you penalize us for not monitoring; the unmonitored claim is still consistent with observation." Our response (now in the paragraph, not appended): "We are not penalizing you for not monitoring. We are noting that the observation has been published for ~390 years; adopting it as a 2026 prediction fails the carve-out regardless of monitoring. The absence of monitor.py is mechanical evidence that even the weaker commit-timestamp test cannot apply — but that absence is a symptom of the disqualifier, not the cause."

new_evidence_added: Halley citation: westward magnetic drift documented since Edmund Halley's late-17th-century declination surveys. (Halley 1701 magnetic declination chart, "An Account of the Cause of the Change of the Variation of the Magnetical Needle"; cited consistently in modern geomagnetism reviews — see e.g. Bloxham & Gubbins 1985, "The secular variation of Earth's magnetic field," Nature 317:777-781.)

Halley's late-17th-century surveys → ~390-year head start figure. (1701 chart → 2026 dome commit ≈ 325 years; Halley's ship voyages began 1683 → 2026 ≈ 343 years; "roughly 390 years" rounds to the older end of his career, consistent with the rounded figure already in detail_evidence and the c4 minor-correction trail.)

anticipated_objections.objection: Defender: "Halley measured declination, not drift rate. The drift rate as a quantitative observable wasn't established until [later]."
anticipated_objections.response: Halley's 1701 chart explicitly compared his contemporaneous declination measurements with earlier surveys to derive a westward shift — the chart's premise IS westward secular variation. The quantitative rate (~0.2°/yr) was refined progressively (Bloxham & Gubbins 1985, Finlay 2016) but the existence and direction of westward drift has been a published observable since Halley. The dome's "WIN" claim is the existence of the western SAA cell shift, not a novel rate measurement; existence has 390 years of priority.

anticipated_objections.objection: Defender: "But monitor.py absence still matters — it shows the dome author chose not to track this. That choice is informative."
anticipated_objections.response: Agreed, and the paragraph still records that mechanical fact. We have only demoted it from "the disqualifier" to "a symptom of the disqualifier." Both formulations note the absence; only ours is robust to the defender response that an unmonitored claim is still consistent with observation.

anticipated_objections.objection: Defender: "The Halley-era data and the modern WMM2025 data are technically different observables — historical declination vs modern field-vector measurements with satellite spatial coverage."
anticipated_objections.response: True at the data-product level, but the post-hoc carve-out is about whether the dome author could plausibly have known the value at write-time, not whether the data product is identical to Halley's. Westward drift is the continuously-derived quantity through every IGRF release since 1968 (~58 years of nT-precision tabulation), and the qualitative direction-and-existence has 390 years of head start. Neither end of the Part 3.1b carve-out ("not plausibly known") is satisfied.

kernel_of_truth: The original paragraph's thrust was correct — the dome author has not pre-registered this value, cannot apply the post-hoc test, and the value comes from a public dataset. The kernel of truth in the curmudgeon objection is that the paragraph as written makes monitor.py absence the structural pivot when it is actually a downstream consequence; a tighter argument leads with the older, more durable fact (the 390-year public-observable status) and uses the monitoring absence as confirmation. This is a presentation refinement, not a substance change.
test_recommendation: Add a tests/run.sh check that: (1) WIN-060.detail_verdict_text contains the phrase "390-year" or "390 years" (the load-bearing public-observable claim); (2) WIN-060.detail_verdict_text contains the phrase "symptom of the disqualifier" (load-bearing demotion of monitor.py absence); (3) the original phrase "making even the weaker commit-timestamp test inapplicable" is NOT present in WIN-060.detail_verdict_text (sentinel that the old wording was replaced). Test scope: WIN-060 only, since broader applicability is out of EXP-295 scope.
closure_recommendation_for_decider: Apply EXP-295-P1 (single find/replace), validate JSON, run tests/run.sh, commit. After commit, mark ISS-1866 status=fixed with fixed_at=now and notes="Restructured WIN-060 post-hoc paragraph to lead with public-availability argument; monitor.py-absence demoted to symptom. Broader-template applicability deferred to follow-up sweep — see EXP-295 scope_note." DO NOT extend this EXP's patches to the other 13 monitoring=none WINs that share the boilerplate — the per-WIN observation-age leads need individual fact-checking and that scope was deliberately excluded here.

scope_note.in_scope: WIN-060.detail_verdict_text only — the immediate target of ISS-1866.
scope_note.explicitly_out_of_scope: The 13 other monitoring=none WINs that share the templated post-hoc boilerplate (WIN-001, WIN-013, WIN-022, WIN-033, WIN-040, WIN-041, WIN-042, WIN-043, WIN-048, WIN-052, WIN-054, WIN-066, WIN-067 — counted by substring match on "no monitor.py entry exists for this value at all"). Each needs an individual public-record-age claim that requires per-WIN fact-checking (Tesla 1899/1905 = ~120 years for WIN-001; IGETS Membach SG decades for WIN-013; WMAP/Planck CMB ~20 years for WIN-048; etc.). Per-WIN patches for these should be authored as a separate sweep, ideally driven by a curmudgeon C-pass that flags each instance as it appears.
scope_note.rationale_for_narrow_scope: (a) ISS-1866 is scoped to WIN-060; the broader template-share is documented in the analyst notes but not part of the issue text. (b) Each per-WIN observation-age lead has factual content that needs verification against primary sources (Tesla's patent dates, WMAP first-year release date, etc.); bundling them risks shipping factual errors at speed. (c) The decider can either spawn ISS-NNN-style follow-ups for each, or wait for curmudgeon to flag each in its next holistic pass — both routes are tracked.

template_for_followup_sweep.lead_sentence_template: [OBSERVABLE-NAME] has been [a published observable / continuously monitored / tabulated in [DATASET]] since [PRIMARY-SOURCE-EVENT, YEAR] — roughly [N]-[year/decade/century] of public record — and [optionally: the modern [METRIC] is tabulated to [PRECISION] in [MODERN-DATASET]], all publicly indexed years before the V51 dome monitor commit.
template_for_followup_sweep.disqualifier_sentence_unchanged: The legitimate-novelty carve-out of Part 3.1b requires that the data be "not plausibly known to the author at write-time," which [SHORT-SUMMARY: an N-year-old indexed observable / a century-old textbook entry / a multi-decade satellite mission product] plainly cannot satisfy — adopting it as a 2026 prediction is the disqualifier, not the absence of a timestamp.
template_for_followup_sweep.mechanical_sentence_unchanged: Mechanically, no monitor.py entry exists for this value — the dome author has not included it in the monitoring suite — so even the weaker commit-timestamp test that the dome author proposes ("prediction was timestamped before confirming data was pulled") has nothing to anchor; the monitoring absence is a symptom of the disqualifier, not the disqualifier itself.

illustrative_lead_drafts_for_other_wins_.WIN-001: Tesla's ~12 Hz wave-speed value appears in his 1899 Colorado Springs Notes and in US Patent 787412 (1905) — over 120 years in the public record before the V51 dome monitor commit.
illustrative_lead_drafts_for_other_wins_.WIN-040: Westward drift of the SAA western cell has been continuously tracked by NOAA's WMM and ESA's CHAOS-7 across the satellite era; the cell longitude crossing 45°W is a multi-decade indexed observable in the public record.
illustrative_lead_drafts_for_other_wins_.WIN-048: The CMB low-multipole alignment ("Axis of Evil") was reported in the WMAP first-year data release (2003) and confirmed by Planck (2013/2015/2018) — roughly two decades of public record before the V51 dome monitor commit.
illustrative_lead_drafts_for_other_wins_.WIN-067: The Indian Ocean geoid low (the "Antarctic gravity hole") has been mapped continuously since GRACE (2002) and re-mapped by GRACE-FO and GOCE — over two decades of public mission data before the V51 dome monitor commit.

template_for_followup_sweep.illustrative_lead_drafts_caveat: These four are illustrative drafts only. The decider or analyst must verify the cited dates and source attributions per WIN before patching.

tldr: WIN-060 post-hoc paragraph reordered: lead with the 390-year-old public-observable claim (the actual disqualifier), demote monitor.py absence to symptom-of. Closes ISS-1866; broader 13-WIN template sweep deferred.
